headlines.title: 7 pedestrians hurt after being hit by vehicle in Oakland
headlines.description: Seven people were injured after being hit by a vehicle in Oakland early Saturday morning, according to the police department. Police said officers were responding to reports of fights near 18th Street and Telegraph Avenue when they received another report regarding a vehicle driving on the sidewalk and striking pedestrians in the 500 block of 18th Street at 1:30 a.m. Authorities said five adults and one juvenile were taken to the hospital and are listed in stable condition. Police added another adult was privately taken to the hospital and is also listed in stable condition. The police department said the driver ran away from the scene. They ask anyone with information to contact their Felony Assault Unit at 510-238-3426. Those who have any videos or photos that could help with the investigation are asked to send them to cidvideos@oaklandca.gov.

headlines.title: 1 dead after wrong way driver causes multi-vehicle crash in Concord
headlines.description: A person died following a multi-vehicle crash involving a wrong way driver in Concord on Friday night, according to the California Highway Patrol. CHP said officers responded to a crash on northbound I-680, north of Treat Boulevard at around 11:20 p.m. Authorities reported a black Charger was traveling wrong way and collided head on with a Tesla. Several other vehicles also crashed into each other, trying to avoid the original collision, according to officials. CHP said a person in the Tesla was declared deceased and the driver in the Charger was taken to the hospital. They add DUI is suspected but not confirmed.

headlines.title: Some East Bay voters will get 2 ballots for the same seat: What to know
headlines.description: Starting Monday, all voters across the Bay Area will get their ballot for California’s June primary. But in Congressional District 14, voters will get another ballot just days later for a special election happening two weeks after that primary. This all stems from a sudden vacancy after longtime Congressman Eric Swalwell resigned following sexual assault allegations, shaking up local politics and triggering a rare special election. Alameda County Supervisor Daniel Haubert says the first ballot, arriving Monday, is for the June primary that will send the top two candidates to November to decide who holds the seat long term. “That will elect the long-term member of Congress,” Haubert said. The second ballot arriving just days later is for a special election two weeks after the primary. That vote will decide who fills the seat right away if someone gets more than 50%. If not, there could be a runoff later this summer. “People are confused about which election should they vote in? Does one nulify the other? Is one more important than the other? Why do we have two elections? Is the most common question,” Haubert said. Officials say the ballots will be different colors to help voters tell them apart. But some say they’ll still need to do their homework. County officials say that special election will cost about $6 million, with the possibility of even more if there’s a runoff.

headlines.title: Mountain View residents can use water again, but with precautions
headlines.description: After a week without drinkable water, Mountain View city officials told residents on Friday they can use water again – but only after taking several steps. The City of Mountain View said those 67 homes near Cuesta Park can finally turn their water back on with precautions. But before using it, the city says people need to flush their plumbing systems. That means running hot water for up to 75 minutes for larger heaters, or until it runs cold, and replacing any water filters. Even after all that, the rule is simple: boil water before drinking or cooking. And there’s no timeline yet for when that requirement will be lifted. The city says it will notify residents once testing confirms the water is bacteria-free. “They’re saying all kinds of things, we gotta flush and this and that. I will not boil water or anything, I will not do anything, until it’s 100% safe by the state,” said Kathy Bonilla, a neighbor. “That sounds a lot of iffy. I’m not boiling water or taking a shower, I’m waiting until my water is clean and tested and ready to go. I’ve waited this long,” said Leslie Edwards, a neighbor. For others, the change brings at least some relief. “We are able to bathe in it, run the dishwasher, and do laundry, which is a really big deal,” said Norman Kitching, a neighbor. City officials say they’ve been testing water samples all week. During Friday’s meeting, they got approval from the state to move into this next phase. It’s been a difficult stretch for residents. The problem started last Friday, when a contractor working on a water main allowed cement slurry to seep into the system, contaminating it with bacteria. “This made it just very, very difficult, you can’t imagine … you can’t even wash your hair,” Bonilla said. Many say they’ve spent extra money on food, laundry, and basic needs. The city now says residents will receive $92 per day for each day affected. “That’s not enough, because I just went to the store, twice,” Bonilla said. The city says if residents need help flushing their plumbing, they can call for assistance. Officials have also filed a claim against the contractor involved.

headlines.title: Moonchild suspends tour after truck with equipment stolen in Oakland
headlines.description: Earlier this week, the truck carrying equipment and merch of Moonchild, a Grammy-nominated band, was stolen in Oakland when they were preparing to perform in Berkeley. They said their U-Haul was parked outside the Best Western Plus Bayside Hotel on Embarcadero in Oakland when someone took it on April 29. The theft happened just before their planned show in Berkeley. “We found a few air tags thrown into bushes, but it seems like they’re pros and they found all of our air tags and just kind of dispersed them throughout town. And yeah, we haven’t been able to recover anything,” said Amber Navran, a Moonchild bandmate. Surveillance video shows a car pulling up, one person getting out and getting into the truck. Moments later, both vehicles drive off with hundreds of thousands of dollars in equipment and merch. The incident forced the band to cancel their four remaining California tour stops. Now, the band says it’s unclear whether they’ll break even from the months-long tour. “Touring has, you know, you have so many expenses. You might, you know, if one thing goes wrong, you might not make any money on a tour. So for something like this to happen was a tough hit for sure,” said Max Bryk, a Moonchild bandmate. The bands members say that, while the cost of what was stolen is in the six figures, the loss goes well beyond money. “We have attachments to these instruments like saxophones and vintage horns, cymbals, you know, just things that we feel like are our personal sound. So, you know, grieving, potentially losing all that stuff is part of it. And then, yeah, just ending the tour on this weird note was a struggle for everyone, you know?” Bryk said. With insurance covering only part of the loss, the band turned to GoFundMe and within a day, more than $43,000 had been raised to try and help make the band whole again. “We are shocked and really, really moved by people’s generosity and love and support,” Navran said. The musicians are no strangers to the Bay Area and understand the risks, but say they’ve never had issues like this before. And despite the abrupt end to their tour, Moonchild says they won’t let this keep them away from the Bay Area or Oakland. “Oakland’s a music town with a lot of music lovers. And so, I definitely want to come back and play shows,” Bryk said. “Yeah, it’s not their fault. I wouldn’t want to put that on anybody, you know. And yeah, our memories of playing in the Bay are just so, so beautiful,” Navran said. While no items have been recovered, the band says they believe OPD has a suspect in custody. Their Bay Area dates are now rescheduled for this summer, and they told NBC Bay Area Mayor Barbara Lee has personally reached out, offering support as the investigation continues.

headlines.title: SF fire chief's top aide in dispute over firefighter union funds
headlines.description: A top aide to the San Francisco fire chief is embroiled in a dispute over “potential” financial irregularities with the firefighters’ union, where she served as treasurer before assuming her current command staff position. Adrienne Sims, a 25-year-veteran firefighter, is currently on stress-related leave from her post as chief of staff to Fire Chief Dean Crispen, which she assumed when he was appointed in January of last year. In a virtual meeting of members of Union Local 798 last week, Sims dismissed the allegations as “nonsense,” but agreed to pay back some of the disputed money. The financial dispute involves union funds, not taxpayer money, union officials say. “There were charges that were incorrect accounting, and I owned it,” she told union members in a video obtained by NBC Bay Area’s investigative unit. Sims doesn’t say how much money is in question, but two sources with direct knowledge of the finances say there’s roughly $50,000 in dispute. Sims did not respond to requests for comment. But told the union meeting last week that a former union official authorized most of the funding in question. Sims says she no longer has access to documentation that could vindicate her and says two thirds of the total involved direct payments authorized by a former union official, who declined comment for this story. “I have willingly – because i want to just make this all go away — offered to pay back any charges that they feel I am responsible for,” she said on the video. She said some of the funds involve a total of $6,000 in tickets to a firefighters’ ball and a separate golf tournament dating back to 2023. In a statement, 798 President Sam Gebler said the union recently “identified potential irregularities involving union finances,” and says the union may refer the matter to “outside authorities.” While offering to pay disputed money back, Sims told the union meeting that she is refusing to sign a letter admitting to misconduct. The fire department said it has not been notified about any civil or criminal allegations involving the union, and should it learn of any, it will refer them to the City Attorney’s office. The department confirmed that Sims is currently on leave. But Sims told union members during the meeting that while she is on stress leave as of April 1, her leave has nothing to do with the financial dispute. She promised to enlist a lawyer in her defense. “It’s unfortunate that I’m being dragged through the mud, my reputation is being dragged through the mud after serving for this local for years,” she said at the meeting, adding: “all of this is nonsense.”

headlines.title: America Startup contest semifinalists gather in San Mateo
headlines.description: Semifinalists of America250’s nationwide contest, which aimed to bring together the country’s future innovators, gathered in San Mateo. The competition was created by America250, a non-partisan group created by Congress to honor the country’s 250th anniversary. “This is an exciting opportunity for them to not just validate what they’re doing but also hear from people who have done it already and really inspire them,” said Rosie Rios, chair of America250. Rios said the goal of America’s Startup was to highlight the next generation of innovators. The best and brightest were invited to submit proposals, and now semifinalists will pitch their ideas to some of the Silicon Valley’s biggest names this weekend. “They are getting ready for a full weekend of activities, including pitching to these top judges that include Chris Larsen the chair of Ripple, Sarah Friar, the CFO of OpenAI and Naveen Jain, a serial entrepreneur, Tim Draper, of course, who put the star in startup and they get be the judges to get down to the final ten winners each of whom will receive $25,000 in non-dilutive grant funding,” Rios said. Christie Smith has the full report in the video player above.

headlines.title: Firefighters battle grass fire in San Jose
headlines.description: Firefighters were battling a grass fire in San Jose on Friday afternoon. The fire happened near Senter and Story roads, not too far from the city’s May Day demonstration, at around 3:40 p.m. NBC Bay Area’s SkyRanger captured aerial footage showing the flames burning up to 3 acres. The fire was knocked down at around 4:30 p.m. No injuries nor damages have been reported.

headlines.title: Passenger robot delays Southwest flight out of Oakland
headlines.description: A humanoid robot named Bebop delayed an Oakland to San Diego flight for nearly an hour while Southwest tried to figure out how to travel with the robot. The robot is made by a company called Elite Event Robotics, which rents out robots like Bebop for events. Bebop can dance and serve drinks. San Jose Apr 22 San Jose Mar 24 Military Feb 24 After an event last week in the Bay Area, Bebop needed to get to Southern California. The new carrying case was too heavy to check in, so Elite’s employee decided to buy the robot a ticket, checked it in and brought the robot onto the plane. Southwest was initially stumped on how to handle the robot, saying the item was essentially a carry-on and therefore could not be in a seat. Ultimately, they moved Bebop from the aisle to the window, took out its battery and the plane went on to its destination.

headlines.title: Man dies in possible self-defense shooting, Benicia police say
headlines.description: A man was killed in a shooting at a home in Benicia late Thursday night, police said. Benicia police and fire crews responded around 10 p.m. to a report of a gunshot at a residence on London Circle. When officers arrived, they found an adult man suffering from a gunshot wound. Emergency crews attempted life-saving measures, including CPR, but the man was pronounced dead at the scene. Police said the call was initially reported as a domestic violence incident and that the shooting might have been in self-defense. All individuals involved live in the same household, including a juvenile. Due to the involvement of a minor, authorities said limited information is being released. The case remains under investigation. If you or someone you know is experiencing domestic violence, call the National Domestic Violence Hotline at (800) 799-SAFE (7233), text “START” to 88788, or call SafeQuest Solano, a local agency that provides confidential Domestic Violence Advocacy/Shelters/Support: 24 Hour Toll free (866) 487-7233

headlines.title: San Jose passenger claims a Waymo drove off with his luggage at the airport
headlines.description: A South Bay man claims a Waymo driverless taxi drove off with his luggage in the trunk after dropping him off at the San Jose Mineta Airport. He has been trying to retrieve his items through Waymo’s customer service, but hasn’t been satisfied with the resolutions they’ve offered so far. On Monday, Di Jin took his first-ever ride in a driverless taxi. He took a Waymo from his home in Sunnyvale to the San Jose Mineta Airport for a business trip. Jin said the ride itself went smoothly. But then, Jin said he got out of the car and went to the trunk to grab his luggage. “I pressed the trunk open button, try to get my luggage, but it doesn’t do anything, and it drives away immediately,” Jin recalled. He said he immediately called Waymo customer service, who told him the vehicle was already on its way back to the depot and could not be turned back around. So Jin flew to San Diego with no suitcase, no change of clothes, and no work notes. Transportation Mar 18 San Francisco Jan 12 California Nov 22, 2025 Later that afternoon, Waymo emailed Jin, letting him know “we have your luggage safely secured at our local depot!” “While we would love to get this item back to you as quickly as possible, Waymo is unable to cover the cost of shipping labels or courier fees,” the support team representative wrote, adding that if Jin prefers not to pay for shipping, there is another option. Waymo also offered to give Jin two complementary Waymo rides with the goal of getting him to and from the depot to pick up his items. Jin didn’t feel it was fair for him to pay for the shipping. He also didn’t feel it was fair for him to take the more than two-hour round trip to San Francisco just to retrieve his items. “It sounds terrible,” Jin said of the options Waymo provided him. “It doesn’t make any sense at all, because it’s not my mistake.” Jin contacted NBC Bay Area when he felt he wasn’t getting anywhere with Waymo’s customer service. We have reached out to Waymo for comment, but have not yet received a statement. On Waymo’s web page about lost and found items, the company says it “is not responsible for items left behind in the vehicle after your trip ends and does not provide refunds or reimbursement for the value of lost items.” Waymo also notes that once a lost item is ready for pickup, it can be retrieved seven days a week at the Waymo depot from 8 a.m. to 6 p.m. Waymo’s web page also notes that the trunk can be opened by pressing the trunk release button on the car or tapping “open trunk” in the Waymo app. “At your destination, the trunk will automatically open when you exit the vehicle,” the web page said. Jin said that the trunk on his ride did not open automatically when he left the Waymo. “I already told them very clearly it’s not lost and found, right? I pressed the trunk open button, and it’s just not functional,” he said. Back in November, San Jose became the first commercial airport in California to offer driverless Waymo rides to paying customers. Jin noted that he is a fan of driverless technology, but said this interaction put a sour taste in his mouth. NBC Bay Area has covered stories before about rideshare customers who claimed their human drivers drove off with their possessions. About a year ago, we interviewed a San Francisco tennis coach who claimed a driverless Waymo took off with his pricey tennis equipment in the trunk. Back then, Waymo told NBC Bay Area, “Waymo’s Support Team operates with the goal of reuniting riders and their forgotten items.” That San Francisco tennis coach maintained that his items were not forgotten.

headlines.title: San Francisco to open RESET Center as jail alternative for addiction recovery
headlines.description: San Francisco is opening a new center aimed at helping people struggling with drug use as an alternative to jail. “RESET,” which stands for Rapid Enforcement, Support, Evaluation and Triage, is part of Mayor Daniel Lurie’s effort to address the fentanyl crisis on city streets. The center is set to begin serving people Monday. Officials said the facility is designed to look and operate more like a healthcare setting than a jail or detention center. It will be run by the San Francisco Sheriff’s Office in partnership with the Department of Public Health and Connections Health Solutions. San Francisco Feb 17 San Francisco Jan 7 According to the mayor’s office, more than 600 people have died from addiction in San Francisco. The sheriff said the center gives law enforcement an option to bring people to a therapeutic environment where they can sober up and choose whether to enter treatment. “We are no longer going to tolerate people using drugs on our streets,” Lurie said. “I’m not going to tolerate it as a father, and I’m not going to tolerate it as a mayor.” Health officials said the center will provide 24/7 nursing care, access to social workers and peer support from people with lived experience. Treatment will not be mandatory, but staff will work to encourage people to seek help. If someone chooses treatment, they can be connected immediately rather than having to find services on their own.
